summary: DHH celebrates a .NET developer named Marten Veldthuis who abandoned his project to port Rails to .NET after giving Ruby on Rails a serious try. Veldthuis found that his development speed matched his ASP.NET/C# productivity almost immediately, even while still learning. DHH uses this as evidence of Rails' superiority and notes that the developer is now considering switching his entire stack from Windows/IIS/MSSQL to FreeBSD/Apache/MySQL, plus buying an Apple laptop.
key_points: A developer building a .NET clone of Rails switched to using Rails itself after trying it seriously on a real project
The developer matched his existing ASP.NET/C# speed almost immediately despite being new to Rails and Ruby
DHH argues Rails productivity compounds over time — fast at first and continually accelerating
The switch from Rails inspired a broader platform migration away from the entire Microsoft stack
money_quotes: Rails makes you go fast really quickly and then keeps on accelerating.
I couldn't think of a nicer ride out of Redmond.
Consider how fast you'll be able to go when you got a week or two of experience under your belt. Then consider how fast you'll be able to go in a month. Then in three months.
I'm thrilled to see that Martin is coming over to the existing Rails community instead of making a language fork.
His adventures with Ruby on Rails have even inspired Martin to think thoughts of FreeBSD, Apache, and MySQL as a replacement for his Windows 2003 with IIS/MSSQL.
key_insight: Rails' productivity advantage is so compelling that a developer building a .NET port of it abandoned the clone in favor of switching his entire technology stack to the original.
